facts.question: Due to general relativity, which part of your body ages slightly slower?
facts.answer: Your feet (they're in a slightly stronger gravitational field)
distractors: Your head (it's farther from Earth's core)
Your hands (they move more, causing time dilation)
Your heart (it's at the center of your body)
facts.explanation: General relativity predicts that time passes slower in stronger gravitational fields. Your feet are closer to Earth's center, experiencing slightly stronger gravity. This has been confirmed with atomic clocks placed at different heights. While 90 nanoseconds is imperceptible, the principle is the same one that requires GPS satellites to correct for relativistic time dilation.

facts.question: How many official time zones does China use despite spanning five geographical ones?
facts.explanation: Mao Zedong established the single time zone in 1949 to promote national unity. This causes practical absurdities — in Xinjiang, the western province, the sun doesn't set until after 10 PM in summer. Many locals unofficially use 'Xinjiang Time' (UTC+6), two hours behind Beijing, in daily life.

facts.question: Are there more trees on Earth or stars in the Milky Way?
facts.answer: More trees on Earth (about 3 trillion vs. 100-400 billion stars)
distractors: More stars in the Milky Way by a factor of 100
More stars by a factor of 10
More trees, but only by a small margin
facts.explanation: A 2015 Yale study using satellite imagery, forest inventories, and supercomputer modeling estimated Earth has 3.04 trillion trees — seven times more than previous estimates. That's about 420 trees per person. However, we're losing about 10 billion trees per year to deforestation.

facts.question: Who first proposed the trolley problem thought experiment in 1967?
facts.explanation: Foot introduced the dilemma of whether to divert a runaway trolley to kill one person instead of five. It became a cornerstone of moral philosophy, illustrating the tension between utilitarian and deontological ethics.

facts.question: During Dutch Tulip Mania in 1637, a single tulip bulb could cost more than how many times a craftsman's annual income?
facts.explanation: Tulip Mania is often cited as one of history's first speculative bubbles. At its peak, rare tulip bulbs traded for 10,000 guilders — enough to buy a grand canal house in Amsterdam. The market crashed abruptly in February 1637.

facts.question: In what year did Alan Turing propose the test for machine intelligence?
facts.explanation: In his paper 'Computing Machinery and Intelligence,' Turing suggested that if a human judge cannot reliably distinguish a machine from a human through text conversation, the machine could be said to 'think.' No machine has definitively passed the test.

facts.question: What is the longest common English word you can type using only the top row of a QWERTY keyboard?
facts.explanation: The top row of a QWERTY keyboard contains Q-W-E-R-T-Y-U-I-O-P. 'Typewriter' uses only T-Y-P-E-W-R-I-T-E-R, all from that row. While there's no definitive proof it was intentional, typewriter historians note that early salesmen needed to quickly demonstrate the machines and having the product's own name easily accessible from one row was convenient.

facts.question: What was unusual about the Remington No. 1 typewriter (1873)?
facts.answer: It typed only uppercase and you couldn't see the text while typing
distractors: It could only type numbers, not letters
It required two people to operate
facts.explanation: The Sholes and Glidden Type-Writer, manufactured by Remington, printed on the underside of the paper. Users had to lift the carriage to check their work. The shift key for lowercase wasn't added until the Remington No. 2 in 1878.

facts.question: What is Usain Bolt's 100-meter world record time, set in 2009?
facts.explanation: Bolt set this record at the 2009 World Championships in Berlin. His peak speed during the race was even higher — estimated at 44.72 km/h (27.8 mph) between the 60m and 80m marks.

facts.question: What inspired George de Mestral to invent Velcro?
facts.answer: Burrs sticking to his dog's fur
distractors: Pine sap bonding to tree bark
facts.explanation: De Mestral examined the burrs under a microscope and discovered tiny hooks on their surface that caught on the loops of fabric and fur. He spent eight years perfecting a synthetic version of this hook-and-loop system, patenting it in 1955.

facts.question: On average, which causes more human deaths per year?
facts.answer: They're roughly comparable — both kill only a handful of people per year
facts.explanation: The comparison is misleading in context: billions of people interact with vending machines daily, making them statistically very safe per encounter. Only a few hundred million people enter the ocean each year. The real takeaway is that shark attacks are extraordinarily rare — you're more likely to be killed by a falling coconut, a cow, or a lightning strike.

facts.question: On which planet does a single day last longer than its entire year?
facts.explanation: Venus also rotates backward compared to most planets (retrograde rotation), so the Sun rises in the west and sets in the east. One theory is that a massive collision billions of years ago flipped Venus upside down. Because of this odd rotation, a solar day on Venus (sunrise to sunrise) is actually about 117 Earth days.

facts.question: What is 'crwth' — one of the longest English words with no standard vowels?
distractors: A type of Scottish whisky barrel
facts.explanation: English borrowed 'crwth' from Welsh, where W functions as a vowel. In strict English rules, it contains no standard vowels (A, E, I, O, U, Y). Other vowel-free words include 'cwtch' (a Welsh cuddle) and 'nth.' The word is pronounced like 'krooth' and refers to a bowed lyre that was popular in medieval Wales and Britain.

facts.question: Where is West Mebon located?
facts.explanation: West Mebon reveals the extraordinary craftsmanship of its builders

facts.question: What was the wheel first used for when it was invented around 3500 BCE?
facts.explanation: The potter's wheel appeared centuries before wheels were attached to carts. The oldest known wheel used for transport dates to around 3200 BCE, found in present-day Slovenia.

facts.question: Does Wi-Fi stand for 'Wireless Fidelity'?
facts.answer: No, it's a made-up brand name inspired by 'Hi-Fi'
distractors: Yes, it stands for Wireless Fidelity
Yes, it stands for Wireless Finder
Yes, it stands for Wide Fiber
No, it stands for Wireless Field
facts.explanation: The Wi-Fi Alliance hired Interbrand to create a catchy name for the IEEE 802.11 protocol. They came up with 'Wi-Fi' as a pun on 'Hi-Fi' (High Fidelity). The Alliance briefly used a tagline 'The Standard for Wireless Fidelity' but later dropped it to avoid confusion.

facts.question: Why do wombats produce cube-shaped droppings?
facts.answer: Varying intestinal wall elasticity molds feces into cubes for territory marking
distractors: They chew their food into perfect square pieces
A special organ called the cuboid compresses waste
Their diet of angular minerals creates the shape
They manually shape their droppings with their paws
facts.explanation: Scientists discovered in 2018 that the cube shape comes from the varying elasticity of the wombat's intestinal walls. The last 8% of the intestine has regions of different stiffness, which mold the feces into cubes. Each wombat produces 80-100 cubes per night. This research won the 2019 Ig Nobel Prize in Physics.

facts.question: What is the world's oldest currency still in use?
facts.explanation: The pound was originally the value of one pound weight of silver pennies. While the currency has evolved dramatically, it has been in continuous use for over 1,200 years.

facts.question: The shortest war in history, lasting about 38–45 minutes, was between which two parties?
facts.explanation: The Anglo-Zanzibar War began at 9:00 AM when the British fleet opened fire on the Sultan's palace. Zanzibar's wooden fleet was destroyed and the palace heavily damaged before a ceasefire was declared, ending the war in under an hour.

facts.question: The Wright Brothers' first flight distance of 120 feet is shorter than what part of a Boeing 747?
facts.explanation: Orville Wright's first flight lasted 12 seconds and traveled 120 feet. A Boeing 747's wingspan is approximately 196–225 feet, meaning the Wright Flyer's entire first flight would have fit within the span of a modern jumbo jet's wings.

facts.question: Why did the Wright Brothers almost never fly together?
facts.answer: Their father made them promise to fly separately in case of a crash
distractors: They had a bitter personal rivalry
The early planes could only carry one person
facts.explanation: Bishop Milton Wright was terrified of losing both sons in a single accident. On May 25, 1910, they finally flew together for the only time, a six-minute flight at Huffman Prairie. Orville then took his 82-year-old father on his first and only airplane flight, during which Milton reportedly shouted 'Higher, Orville, higher!'

facts.question: Where is Yue Fei Temple located?
facts.explanation: Yue Fei Temple offers a window into a fascinating lost civilization

facts.question: Who first defined zero as a number with arithmetic rules around the 5th–7th century?
facts.explanation: While the Babylonians and Maya used zero as a placeholder, Brahmagupta in 628 AD was the first to define zero as a number with its own arithmetic rules, including operations like adding and subtracting zero.

facts.question: What is Zeus Sosipolis Temple?
facts.answer: Ancient greek temple in magnesia on the meander
facts.explanation: Zeus Sosipolis Temple represents the pinnacle of ancient Greek religious architecture. Greek temples were designed to house deity statues and served as focal points for religious festivals and civic ceremonies.

facts.question: What is Zhuqing Temple?
facts.explanation: Zhuqing Temple embodies Buddhist devotion and artistic expression. Buddhist temples serve as centers of meditation, learning, and community, often featuring ornate decorations and serene gardens.

facts.question: Who perfected the modern zipper design in 1913?
facts.explanation: Whitcomb Judson patented a 'clasp locker' in 1893, but it was unreliable. Sundbäck redesigned it with interlocking teeth and increased the number of elements per inch, creating the zipper we know today.

facts.question: What does GLTP stand for in human genetics?
facts.explanation: GLTP is one of thousands of protein-coding genes that make you who you are!

facts.question: What does GAPDH enzyme do in cells?
facts.explanation: GAPDH is the hardest-working enzyme—it powers trillions of cells!

facts.question: What type of gene is GPC4?
facts.explanation: Your cells contain thousands of genes like GPC4 working silently to build you!

facts.question: What type of gene is GPC5 in humans?
facts.explanation: Every gene in your body is a blueprint for building proteins!

facts.question: What does GPRC5D encode in human cells?
facts.explanation: GPRC5D helps your cells receive signals through a unique receptor protein!

facts.question: Who won the Battle of Grunwald in 1410?
facts.explanation: One of medieval Europe's largest battles ended Teutonic dominance forever!

facts.question: When was Guang Ming Temple in Orlando founded?
facts.explanation: A modern Buddhist sanctuary bringing Eastern spirituality to Central Florida!

facts.question: What does 3-D mammography image?
facts.explanation: 3-D mammography finds up to 40% more cancers than traditional 2-D!

facts.question: What does the ACHE enzyme break down?
facts.explanation: After a nerve signal fires, acetylcholine must be rapidly cleared from the synapse so the next signal can be clean and precise. ACHE breaks it down in milliseconds. Nerve agents and some pesticides work by blocking ACHE, causing uncontrolled nerve firing. Alzheimer's drugs also target this enzyme to boost brain signaling.

facts.question: What does the ADAMTS5 enzyme break down?
facts.explanation: ADAMTS5 is the primary aggrecanase — an enzyme that chews through aggrecan, the main protein keeping cartilage springy and cushioning. In osteoarthritis, ADAMTS5 becomes overactive and destroys cartilage faster than the body can repair it.

facts.question: What does caffeine block to keep you awake?
facts.explanation: Adenosine builds up in the brain during waking hours and makes you feel sleepy by binding to A2A receptors. Caffeine molecules are shaped similarly to adenosine and block these receptors, preventing the sleepy signal. This is why coffee helps you stay alert.

facts.question: What does agrin organize in the body?
facts.explanation: Without agrin, the receptors on muscle cells would scatter randomly, making nerve-to-muscle communication impossible. Agrin clusters acetylcholine receptors at the junction, ensuring efficient signal transmission. Disruption of agrin function contributes to muscle weakness in conditions like myasthenia gravis.

facts.question: What does the AMPD1 enzyme do in muscles?
facts.explanation: AMPD1 converts AMP to IMP in the purine nucleotide cycle, which is critical for maintaining energy levels in working muscles. Deficiency of this enzyme is one of the most common metabolic muscle disorders, causing fatigue and cramps after exercise.

facts.question: What triggers an animal allergy?
facts.explanation: Cat allergen particles are so tiny they can float in the air for hours!

facts.question: What makes ASSR different from traditional hearing tests?
facts.answer: It works on patients who cannot respond
facts.explanation: ASSR works by playing rapid clicking sounds into the ear and measuring the brain's electrical response using electrodes on the scalp. Unlike traditional hearing tests that require a response, ASSR can test hearing in babies, unconscious patients, or anyone who cannot respond voluntarily. It provides frequency-specific hearing thresholds.

facts.question: How is autism diagnosed?
facts.explanation: Autism spectrum disorder is identified through careful observation of social communication, repetitive behaviors, and sensory responses. Specialists use standardized tools like the ADOS-2 (Autism Diagnostic Observation Schedule). Early diagnosis, often possible by age 2, leads to better outcomes through early intervention therapies.

facts.question: What does autoinflation treat?
facts.answer: Blocked Eustachian tubes and middle ear fluid
facts.explanation: When the Eustachian tube is blocked (often from colds or allergies), fluid builds up in the middle ear causing muffled hearing. Autoinflation involves blowing up a special balloon with one nostril, which forces air up the tube and equalizes pressure. It is a simple, non-surgical alternative to ear tubes.

facts.question: What is autoinoculation?
facts.answer: Self-spreading an infection to another body part
facts.explanation: Common examples include spreading warts from one hand to the other, or transferring cold sore virus from the lips to the eyes. Hand washing is the simplest prevention. Some skin conditions like molluscum contagiosum spread mainly through autoinoculation.

facts.question: What does BACE1-AS increase production of?
facts.explanation: BACE1-AS is an antisense RNA — it does not make protein but instead stabilizes the messenger RNA of BACE1, boosting production of the beta-secretase enzyme. This enzyme cuts amyloid precursor protein into toxic fragments that clump into the brain plaques seen in Alzheimer's. Researchers hope blocking BACE1-AS could slow the disease.

facts.question: What chemical in blister beetles causes skin blistering?
facts.explanation: Cantharidin causes blistering of the skin within hours of contact. The beetles do not bite or sting — simply crushing one against the skin releases the toxin. The blisters are painful but usually heal without scarring within a week or two.

facts.question: What is a bone neoplasm?
facts.explanation: Your bones are alive and constantly rebuilding themselves throughout your life!

facts.question: What organism causes candidemia?
facts.explanation: Candida species live on your skin and in your gut normally without harm!

facts.question: What happens in a carotid artery dissection?
facts.explanation: The carotid arteries carry blood from the heart to the brain. A dissection creates a false channel in the artery wall where blood collects, narrowing or blocking the vessel. This can cause strokes even in young, healthy adults and may be triggered by neck injuries or chiropractic manipulation.

facts.question: Why is CD33 important in leukemia treatment?
facts.answer: It is a target on leukemia cells for drug delivery
facts.explanation: CD33 sits on myeloid immune cells like monocytes and acts as a receptor that can dampen immune activation. Because leukemia cells often display CD33, doctors use antibody drugs that target CD33 to deliver chemotherapy directly to cancer cells.

facts.question: What do cerebral embolic protection devices catch?
facts.explanation: During procedures like heart valve replacement, tiny fragments of tissue or clot can break loose and travel to the brain, causing a stroke. Embolic protection devices are small basket-like filters inserted into the brain's arteries to catch these fragments. After the procedure, the filter is removed along with the captured debris.

facts.question: Where does cerebral hemorrhage occur?
facts.explanation: The brain uses 20% of your blood supply despite being only 2% of body weight!

facts.question: How is a baby delivered via C-section?
facts.explanation: About 1 in 3 babies in the US is born via C-section!

facts.question: What causes chemically induced disorders?
facts.explanation: Your liver is your body's chemical detox factory, filtering 1.5 liters of blood per minute!

facts.question: What was a clyster used for in historical medicine?
facts.explanation: Clysters were large metal or ivory syringes that injected liquid into the rectum for supposed health benefits. They were so popular that King Louis XIV of France reportedly received over 2,000 clysters during his lifetime. The practice was eventually abandoned as medical science advanced.

facts.question: What protects the brain from most bacterial infections?
facts.explanation: The CNS is normally protected by the blood-brain barrier, which keeps most pathogens out. When bacteria breach this barrier, infections like bacterial meningitis can develop rapidly and become life-threatening. Early antibiotic treatment is critical for survival.

facts.question: What is a comedone?
facts.answer: A clogged skin pore (blackhead or whitehead)
facts.explanation: Comedones form when hair follicles become clogged with dead skin cells and oil. A dermatologist uses a comedone extractor — a small metal tool with a loop — to apply gentle pressure around the clogged pore, pushing out the plug. This is safer than squeezing with fingers, which can cause scarring and infection.

facts.question: What happens in a comminuted fracture?
facts.explanation: High-energy impacts like car crashes commonly cause comminuted fractures!

facts.question: What is complete edentulism?
facts.explanation: Sharks grow unlimited new teeth, but humans only get two sets for life!

facts.question: What does congenital torticollis cause?
facts.explanation: Babies with torticollis often prefer looking in one direction only!

facts.question: What virus does a COVID-19 test detect?
facts.explanation: COVID-19 PCR tests can detect just a few virus particles in a sample!

facts.question: What is the purpose of cricoid pressure?
facts.explanation: Also called the Sellick maneuver, cricoid pressure compresses the esophagus against the vertebral column. It was traditionally used during emergency intubation to reduce the risk of aspiration. Recent evidence has questioned its effectiveness.

facts.question: What becomes exposed in dental pulp exposure?
facts.explanation: Tooth enamel is the hardest substance in your body, even harder than bone!

facts.question: What direction do fragments go in depressed skull fracture?
facts.explanation: The human skull is made of 22 bones fused together for maximum protection!

facts.question: How much brain area does diffuse injury affect?
facts.explanation: Your brain has about 86 billion neurons that can be harmed by diffuse injury!

facts.question: What neurotransmitter does the DRD3 receptor respond to?
facts.explanation: Dopamine receptors in the brain respond to the neurotransmitter dopamine, influencing reward, movement, and emotion. The D3 subtype is concentrated in the limbic system, which processes emotions. Antipsychotic medications often target D3 receptors to help manage symptoms of schizophrenia and addiction.

facts.question: What does the 'C' stand for in the RICE protocol?
facts.explanation: Made of woven elastic fabric, these bandages stretch to apply consistent pressure around an injury. They are a key part of the RICE protocol (Rest, Ice, Compression, Elevation) for sprains and strains. Wrapping too tightly can cut off circulation, so you should always be able to slip a finger underneath.

facts.question: What causes elbow tendinopathy pain?
facts.explanation: Tennis elbow got its name in 1883 but most sufferers have never played tennis!

facts.question: What gets inflamed in endarteritis?
facts.explanation: If you lined up all your blood vessels end to end, they'd circle Earth twice!

facts.question: What is an endoscopic stent used for?
facts.explanation: Stents are tiny mesh or plastic tubes inserted through an endoscope — a flexible camera tube passed through the mouth or other opening. They are used to open blocked bile ducts, esophagus, or airways. The procedure avoids large surgical incisions and patients often go home the same day.

facts.question: Which organ produces erythropoietin (EPO)?
facts.explanation: When oxygen levels drop — such as at high altitude — the kidneys release more EPO, stimulating red blood cell production. This increases oxygen-carrying capacity. EPO is also used medically to treat anemia and has been infamously misused as a performance-enhancing drug in sports.

facts.question: What does esophageal dilation widen?
facts.explanation: Your esophagus is about 10 inches long and uses muscles to push food down!

facts.question: What replaces the esophagus after an esophagectomy?
facts.answer: The stomach is pulled up into the chest
facts.explanation: During an esophagectomy, the removed section of esophagus is replaced by pulling the stomach up into the chest or using a section of intestine to create a new food tube. It is one of the most complex operations in surgery, taking 4-8 hours. Patients must relearn how to eat in smaller portions afterward.

facts.question: What two injuries occur in fracture dislocation?
facts.explanation: The ankle is the most common site for fracture dislocations!

facts.question: What causes frostbite?
facts.explanation: Frostbite can occur in just 30 minutes at minus 15 degrees Fahrenheit!

facts.question: What characterizes hallucinosis?
facts.explanation: Your brain creates all your experiences, so hallucinations feel completely real!

facts.question: How many bones are in the human hand?
facts.explanation: Unlike congenital deformities present at birth, acquired hand deformities result from fractures healing incorrectly, arthritis warping joints, tendon injuries, burns, or nerve damage. The hand has 27 bones, 29 joints, and over 120 ligaments, making it vulnerable to many types of deformity.

facts.question: What does the head tilt chin lift do?
facts.explanation: This simple move can save a life by keeping the tongue from blocking air!

facts.question: What does heavy metal detox remove?
facts.explanation: The word chelation comes from Greek for claw because it grabs metal ions!

facts.question: What mineral is lacking in hypophosphatemic rickets?
facts.explanation: Rickets was so common in 1600s England it was called the English disease!

facts.question: What causes an insulin coma?
facts.explanation: Your brain uses about 120 grams of glucose daily, more than any other organ!

facts.question: What type of immune response does IL-4 promote?
facts.explanation: IL-4 is produced by T-helper cells and promotes the production of IgE antibodies, which are central to allergic responses. It also activates eosinophils and mast cells to fight parasitic infections. Overproduction of IL-4 contributes to asthma, hay fever, and eczema.

facts.question: What is an intestinal fistula?
facts.explanation: The human intestine is about 25 feet long, creating many fistula sites!

facts.question: What amino acid does the IVD enzyme help break down?
facts.explanation: Isovaleryl-CoA dehydrogenase (IVD) is essential in the metabolic pathway that breaks down leucine from digested proteins. When this enzyme is deficient, toxic compounds build up causing isovaleric acidemia — a metabolic disorder that can make a baby's sweat smell like sweaty feet. Newborn screening can detect it early.

facts.question: What role does the KIF11 protein play in cells?
facts.explanation: KIF11 encodes a kinesin motor protein that walks along microtubules to separate chromosomes during mitosis. Mutations in KIF11 can cause microcephaly with abnormal brain development. Cancer researchers study it because blocking KIF11 stops tumor cells from dividing.

facts.question: What do KRIT1 mutations cause in the brain?
facts.explanation: KRIT1 protein helps blood vessel cells maintain proper junctions and structure. When mutated, blood vessels form raspberry-like clusters that can leak or bleed, especially in the brain. These cavernous malformations affect about 1 in 200 people, though most never know they have them.

facts.question: What does Lamaze focus on during birth?
facts.explanation: Dr. Lamaze brought this method from Russia to France in the 1950s!

facts.question: What system does lead poisoning damage?
facts.explanation: Ancient Romans unknowingly poisoned themselves with lead water pipes!

facts.question: How many sounds are used in the Ling sounds test?
facts.explanation: Developed by audiologist Daniel Ling, this simple test covers the full range of speech sounds from low frequency (/m/) to high frequency (/s/). Parents and teachers can perform it daily to check hearing aid or cochlear implant function. If a child cannot detect one of the six sounds, it indicates a gap in their hearing at that frequency.

facts.question: What does a lumbar puncture collect?
facts.explanation: Cerebrospinal fluid cushions your brain like a built-in shock absorber!

facts.question: What goes wrong in a malunited fracture?
facts.explanation: Bones naturally try to heal themselves within about 6 to 8 weeks!

facts.question: Which bone is affected in mandibular injury?
facts.explanation: Your jaw can exert up to 200 pounds of force when biting down!

facts.question: Which bone breaks in a maxillary fracture?
facts.explanation: The maxilla is actually two bones fused together at the center of your face!

facts.question: What does a mutation near MCM6 allow some adults to do?
facts.explanation: Most mammals stop producing lactase (the milk-digesting enzyme) after weaning. A regulatory region within the MCM6 gene controls lactase persistence in humans. About 35% of the world's population carries mutations that keep lactase production going, allowing them to drink milk as adults without digestive problems.

facts.question: What characterizes microcephaly?
facts.explanation: Primary microcephaly is present at birth and can be caused by genetic mutations. The brain does not grow to its normal size, which may lead to developmental delays. Some forms are inherited in an autosomal recessive pattern.

facts.question: When do mouth abnormalities develop?
facts.explanation: About 1 in 700 babies is born with a cleft lip or palate worldwide!

facts.question: Where is the MT-TH gene located?
facts.explanation: MT-TH encodes a transfer RNA for the amino acid histidine within the mitochondria. Mitochondria have their own small circular genome inherited only from the mother. Mutations in MT-TH can cause hearing loss and other mitochondrial disorders.

facts.question: What is the function of the nebulin protein in muscles?
facts.explanation: Nebulin acts as a molecular ruler inside muscle cells, determining how long the thin filaments (actin) should be. At over 6,000 amino acids long, it is a giant protein. Mutations in the NEB gene are the most common cause of nemaline myopathy, a muscle weakness disorder.

facts.question: What happens during nerve degeneration?
facts.explanation: Nerve signals can travel up to 268 mph, faster than a race car!

facts.question: What structure does occludin help form between cells?
facts.explanation: Occludin is a key component of tight junctions — the molecular zippers that seal neighboring cells together. These junctions are critical in the gut lining, blood-brain barrier, and skin. When occludin is disrupted, barriers become leaky, contributing to conditions like inflammatory bowel disease.

facts.question: What is an oesophagotomy?
facts.explanation: The oesophagus (esophagus) is the muscular tube connecting the throat to the stomach. When an object becomes lodged and cannot be removed endoscopically, surgeons may cut into the oesophagus directly. The procedure requires careful closure to prevent leaking.

facts.question: What is oneirism?
facts.explanation: Unlike normal dreaming during sleep, oneirism blurs the boundary between waking and dreaming states. It can be triggered by fever, certain medications, or neurological conditions. The term comes from the Greek word 'oneiros' meaning dream.

facts.question: What can optic nerve injury cause?
facts.explanation: Each optic nerve contains about one million nerve fibers carrying vision data!

facts.question: What is oral hemorrhage?
facts.explanation: The mouth has more blood vessels per area than almost any other body part!

facts.question: What does ototoxicity damage?
facts.explanation: The tiny hair cells in your inner ear cannot regrow once damaged!

facts.question: What does oxygen insufflation deliver?
facts.explanation: Every cell in your body needs oxygen to produce energy and stay alive!

facts.question: What does a pacemaker regulate?
facts.explanation: Modern pacemakers are smaller than a coin and last over 10 years!

facts.question: What does a positive patellar tap test indicate?
facts.explanation: Normally, the kneecap sits snugly on the femur. When the knee joint fills with excess fluid (effusion) from injury or arthritis, the kneecap floats upward. Pressing it down causes a distinctive tap or bounce as it hits the bone through the fluid layer. This simple bedside test helps doctors diagnose knee problems.

facts.question: Where is the PCDH11Y gene located in the human genome?
facts.explanation: PCDH11Y encodes protocadherin 11 Y-linked, a cell adhesion molecule active in the brain. Because it sits on the Y chromosome, only males carry it. Some researchers have studied whether it plays a role in language development.

facts.question: What is the primary purpose of a pelvic binder?
facts.answer: Stabilize pelvic fractures and reduce bleeding
facts.explanation: Pelvic fractures from car accidents or falls can cause massive internal bleeding because large blood vessels run through the pelvis. A pelvic binder squeezes the broken bones together, reducing the pelvic volume and tamponading the bleeding. It can be applied in minutes by emergency responders.

facts.question: What does pericardiocentesis drain?
facts.explanation: The pericardium is a thin sac that normally contains a small amount of lubricating fluid around the heart. When too much fluid accumulates (cardiac tamponade), it can squeeze the heart and prevent it from pumping. Pericardiocentesis uses a needle guided by ultrasound to drain the fluid and restore heart function.

facts.question: What does the urokinase enzyme dissolve?
facts.explanation: Urokinase converts plasminogen into plasmin, which breaks down fibrin — the mesh that holds blood clots together. Doctors use urokinase to treat dangerous blood clots in the lungs, legs, and heart. The PLAU gene is also studied in cancer because tumor cells hijack urokinase to invade surrounding tissues.

facts.question: What does pleurodesis prevent?
facts.explanation: Between the lung and chest wall is a thin space called the pleural cavity. In conditions like recurrent pneumothorax, air keeps leaking into this space, causing the lung to collapse. Pleurodesis introduces a substance (like talc) that irritates the surfaces, causing them to stick together permanently and eliminating the space where air can collect.

facts.question: What are the key features of poikiloderma?
facts.answer: Thinning skin, color changes, visible blood vessels
facts.explanation: The name comes from Greek words meaning 'varied skin.' Poikiloderma shows a distinctive mix of redness, brown pigmentation, and pale patches with fine spider-like blood vessels. It can result from sun damage, radiation, or autoimmune diseases.

facts.question: What does the Ponseti method treat?
facts.explanation: The Ponseti method has a 95% success rate for treating clubfoot!

facts.question: Which heart valve narrows in pulmonic stenosis?
facts.explanation: Your heart beats about 100,000 times per day, pumping through four valves!

facts.question: What does pulse oximetry measure?
facts.explanation: A pulse oximeter reads your oxygen level in seconds using red light!

facts.question: What causes Rapunzel syndrome?
facts.answer: Swallowing hair that forms an intestinal hairball
facts.explanation: Named after the fairy tale character with extremely long hair, this condition occurs when compulsive hair-eating (trichophagia) creates a large hairball called a trichobezoar. The 'tail' extending into the intestines distinguishes it from a simple stomach hairball. Surgical removal is usually required.

facts.question: When does REM parasomnia occur?
facts.explanation: Your brain paralyzes your muscles during dreams so you don't act them out!

facts.question: What does a sestamibi scan help locate?
facts.explanation: The parathyroid glands are four tiny glands behind the thyroid that control calcium levels. When one becomes overactive (hyperparathyroidism), it causes high blood calcium. The sestamibi scan uses a mildly radioactive tracer that is absorbed more by overactive parathyroid tissue, allowing surgeons to locate the problem gland before surgery.

facts.question: Who does severe acute malnutrition mainly affect?
facts.explanation: Ready-to-use therapeutic food has saved millions of malnourished children worldwide!

facts.question: What does spinal osteochondrosis affect?
facts.explanation: About 80% of people will experience back pain at some point in their lives!

facts.question: What does spine trauma affect?
facts.explanation: Your spine supports your entire body weight while staying flexible enough to bend!

facts.question: What does tenoplasty repair?
facts.explanation: Tendons are the tough, fibrous cords that connect muscles to bones. When tendons are torn or degenerated beyond self-healing, surgeons perform tenoplasty to stitch, graft, or reconstruct them. Recovery typically involves weeks of immobilization followed by physical therapy.

facts.question: What is the most common type of headache?
facts.explanation: Unlike migraines, tension headaches usually affect both sides of the head and do not cause nausea or light sensitivity. They are often triggered by stress, poor posture, or lack of sleep. Most people experience them at some point in their lives.

facts.question: Which vitamin is thiamine?
facts.explanation: Japanese navy doctor Kanehiro Takaki discovered thiamine prevents beriberi in 1884!

facts.question: What does TMJ stand for in medicine?
facts.explanation: The TMJ is one of the most complex joints in the body, allowing you to chew, talk, and yawn. TMJ disorders can cause clicking, pain, and difficulty opening the mouth. Stress-related teeth grinding is a common contributing factor.

facts.question: What causes tooth demineralization?
facts.explanation: Your saliva naturally repairs minor tooth demineralization every day!

facts.question: How does a tourniquet stop bleeding?
facts.answer: By compressing the artery to stop blood flow
facts.explanation: Modern tourniquets are simple devices that can be applied by anyone in an emergency. They work by compressing the artery against the bone, completely stopping blood flow below the tourniquet. While they were once feared for causing limb damage, research shows they can safely remain in place for several hours.

facts.question: Where is a tube placed during intubation?
facts.explanation: Intubation tubes are placed in seconds by skilled emergency doctors!

facts.question: What does the UBA1 enzyme activate?
facts.explanation: Ubiquitin is a small protein that gets attached to other proteins as a 'destroy me' tag. UBA1 activates ubiquitin so it can be attached to targets. Mutations in UBA1 cause VEXAS syndrome, a recently discovered inflammatory disease affecting blood and immune cells.

facts.question: What causes vitamin D deficiency?
facts.explanation: About one billion people worldwide are deficient in vitamin D!

facts.question: What does whole-body MRI scan?
facts.explanation: An MRI magnet is about 60,000 times stronger than Earth's field!

facts.question: What does the HIS3 gene encode?
facts.explanation: HIS3 is a molecular building block for essential amino acids your body needs!

facts.question: Which came first: Oxford University or the Aztec Empire?
facts.gaiaComment: Oxford predates the Aztec Empire. While students were failing exams in England, the Aztecs hadn't been invented yet.
facts.explanation: Oxford's university teaching evolved gradually from the late 11th century, with significant growth after 1167. The Aztec (Mexica) people founded Tenochtitlan on a lake island in 1325 and built their empire over the next two centuries, reaching its height by 1500 before the Spanish conquest in 1521.

facts.question: How long did the Anglo-Zanzibar War last?
facts.gaiaComment: Thirty-eight minutes. The shortest war ever fought. Some meetings last longer.
facts.explanation: Zanzibar's sultan Khalid bin Barghash seized power without British approval. Britain issued an ultimatum, Zanzibar refused, and the British navy opened fire. The Zanzibari forces, outnumbered and outgunned, surrendered within 45 minutes. Casualties: 500 Zanzibaris, 1 British sailor wounded.

facts.question: Can the Great Wall of China be seen from space naked eye?
facts.gaiaComment: The Great Wall is not visible from space. Sorry. Astronauts checked. The myth is better, but no.
facts.explanation: The Great Wall averages 4–9 metres wide — roughly the width of a highway. Visual resolution from the International Space Station (about 400 km altitude) requires objects to be much wider. Astronauts can see roads and cities because of reflected light, but the Wall's narrow profile and colour blend with the surrounding terrain.

facts.question: What was Napoleon's actual height in modern measurement?
facts.gaiaComment: Napoleon wasn't short. British propaganda did that. He was average height. The propaganda was better PR than the truth.
facts.explanation: Napoleon's reported height in French inches was 5 pieds 2 pouces — French units slightly larger than English ones. Converted to modern measurement, he stood about 168–170 cm, close to the French male average of the time. British caricaturists, particularly James Gillray, popularised the 'Little Boney' image as wartime propaganda.

facts.question: How was the Library of Alexandria most likely destroyed?
facts.gaiaComment: The Library of Alexandria wasn't destroyed in one fire. It just... faded away over centuries. History is boring like that.
facts.explanation: The Library of Alexandria suffered multiple incidents: Julius Caesar's fire in 48 BC (likely affecting a warehouse, not the main library), Aurelian's attack in 270s AD, Theophilus's destruction of the Serapeum in 391 AD, and the Arab conquest in 641 AD. Each contributed to its decline. No single event destroys a library containing hundreds of thousands of scrolls.

facts.question: What was originally green about Coca-Cola?
facts.gaiaComment: The bottles were green, not the drink. One letter of clarification away from being less interesting.
facts.explanation: Early Coca-Cola was bottled in green glass because the specific silica and minerals available to glassmakers at the time naturally produced a green tint. The distinctive contour bottle design (1916), often in green glass, helped distinguish Coke from imitators. The drink itself has always been brown.

facts.question: What was the first computer bug in 1947?
facts.answer: A moth in Harvard Mark II
facts.gaiaComment: The first computer bug was an actual moth. Taped into the log. Still at the Smithsonian. Peak documentation.
facts.explanation: On September 9, 1947, technicians working on the Harvard Mark II found a moth stuck in Relay #70, Panel F, causing a malfunction. The term 'bug' for a technical error had been used in engineering before, but this incident popularised it in computing. Grace Hopper, who worked on the team, is often credited with coining the term.

facts.question: Why did ancient Romans use urine as toothpaste?
facts.gaiaComment: Urine contains ammonia. Romans used it as toothpaste. The Emperor taxed the trade. Entrepreneurship.
facts.explanation: Urine was collected from public latrines in ancient Rome and used by fullers (cloth cleaners) and dental practitioners. The urea in urine breaks down into ammonia, which acts as a mild bleach and abrasive cleaner. This practice was not unique to Rome — it appeared across many ancient cultures.

facts.question: Where did the Vikings-with-horned-helmets myth originate?
facts.gaiaComment: Horned Viking helmets came from an opera costume designer in 1876. Halloween owes an apology to Scandinavia.
facts.explanation: Archaeological evidence shows Viking helmets were simple conical or domed iron forms, sometimes with a nose guard. The only near-complete Viking helmet found (the Gjermundbu helmet, Norway) has no horns. Horned helmets did exist in Scandinavia but from the Bronze Age — 2,000 years before the Viking era — used for ceremonies, not battle.

facts.question: In what year was the guillotine last used in France?
facts.gaiaComment: The guillotine and Star Wars existed at the same time. France. 1977. Let that land.
facts.explanation: France was the last country in Western Europe to abolish capital punishment, doing so in 1981 under President Mitterrand. The guillotine, invented as a humane method of execution in the French Revolution, remained the legal execution method until then. Djandoubi was convicted of torture and murder.

facts.question: How many confirmed deaths from the Great Fire of London, 1666?
facts.gaiaComment: The Great Fire of London: six confirmed deaths. Thirteen thousand houses. History is strange.
facts.explanation: The Great Fire burned from 2–6 September 1666, starting in a bakery on Pudding Lane. Despite enormous destruction, contemporary accounts record only six confirmed deaths — though historians suspect the true number was higher, particularly among the poor who are less well-documented. The fire fundamentally reshaped London's architecture toward brick and stone.

facts.question: What antimicrobial did ancient Egyptians apply to infected wounds?
facts.gaiaComment: Ancient Egyptians packed wounds with moldy bread. Three thousand years before penicillin. Empirical medicine before microscopes.
facts.explanation: Egyptian medical papyri, including the Ebers Papyrus (c. 1550 BC), describe applying mouldy bread to infections. The Penicillium mould that naturally grows on bread produces penicillin. This is an early example of empirical medicine — treatments discovered to work before the underlying mechanism was understood.

facts.question: What did Caesar do after pirates ransomed him?
facts.gaiaComment: Caesar joked he'd crucify the pirates. Then he did. When he said something, he meant it.
facts.explanation: This incident occurred around 75 BC when Caesar was about 25. Plutarch and Suetonius both describe his behaviour as surprisingly relaxed — he participated in games and writing with his captors while the ransom was gathered. After paying 50 talents and securing his freedom, he swiftly organised a naval force, captured the pirates, and executed them as promised.

facts.question: How much taller does the Eiffel Tower grow in summer?
facts.gaiaComment: The Eiffel Tower grows 15 centimeters in summer. Iron expands. The tower literally knows what season it is.
facts.explanation: Thermal expansion is a property of all metals — as temperature rises, atomic vibrations increase and material expands. The Eiffel Tower's iron structure responds visibly to temperature differences between winter and summer in Paris, which can range by 40°C or more. The effect is measurable but not visible to the naked eye.

facts.question: What was a Roman thermopolium?
facts.gaiaComment: Romans had fast food restaurants with heated counters 2,000 years ago. Civilization peaks, collapses, and reinvents the same things.
facts.explanation: Thermopolia (singular: thermopolium) were ubiquitous in Roman cities. Most Romans lived in apartment blocks (insulae) with no cooking facilities, so street food was essential. Excavated thermopolia in Pompeii show colourful frescoes advertising their menus and preserved food residue in the dolia — including duck, pork, fish, and snails.

facts.question: What happened to Einstein's brain after his 1955 death?
facts.gaiaComment: A pathologist stole Einstein's brain, kept it in a jar for decades, and mailed slices to researchers. Science is wild.
facts.explanation: Harvey believed Einstein's brain held the secret to genius and kept it against the wishes of Einstein's family. He lost his medical licence, moved multiple times, and kept the brain throughout. Subsequent research found structural differences in the parietal lobes — regions associated with mathematical reasoning — though interpretation remains contested.

facts.question: Were Great Pyramid workers enslaved or paid?
facts.gaiaComment: The pyramid builders got beer, bread, and medical care. They left graffiti. They named their teams. They were people.
facts.explanation: Archaeological excavations by Zahi Hawass and Mark Lehner found workers' towns with bakeries, breweries, medical facilities, and cemeteries near the pyramids. Skeletons show signs of surgery and healed fractures. Graffiti inscriptions from work teams survive on casing stones. The 'enslaved builders' myth dates to Herodotus and was later popularised by films.

facts.question: What is humanity's oldest known written recipe for?
facts.gaiaComment: Humanity's oldest written recipe is for beer. Priorities haven't changed much in four thousand years.
facts.explanation: The Hymn to Ninkasi, addressed to the Sumerian goddess of beer, contains detailed instructions for malting barley, making bread-like bappir used as a fermentation base, and brewing. Archaeological evidence suggests beer brewing dates back even further — to at least 3400 BC — based on residues found in ancient vessels.

facts.question: What knotted string system did Incas use for records?
facts.gaiaComment: The Inca ran an empire spanning 4,000 kilometers with no writing system. Just knots. Magnificently efficient knots.
facts.explanation: Quipus (or khipus) are systems of coloured, knotted strings that recorded numerical data and possibly narratives. They were used for census data, tax records, calendars, and possibly histories. The Inca trained specialist keepers called quipucamayoc. Modern scholars have partially deciphered the numerical system but the full code remains mysterious.

facts.question: How did Black Death labour scarcity help end feudalism?
facts.answer: Peasants gained bargaining power for wages
facts.gaiaComment: One-third of Europe died. The survivors got to negotiate wages for the first time. Silver linings are complicated.
facts.explanation: The Black Death (1347–1351) killed an estimated 30–60% of Europe's population. With a labour shortage, landowners competed for surviving workers, driving up wages and weakening the feudal obligation system. Some historians consider it a pivotal moment in the transition from medieval to early modern Europe.

facts.question: What was the 'War of Currents' between Edison and Tesla?
facts.answer: Rivalry over AC versus DC electricity
facts.gaiaComment: Edison electrocuted an elephant to scare people away from AC power. Your wall socket is Tesla's legacy. Edison lost.
facts.explanation: In the 1880s–90s, Edison promoted DC and Tesla (backed by Westinghouse) promoted AC. Edison staged public demonstrations of animal electrocutions using AC to claim it was dangerous. AC ultimately won because it can be transmitted over long distances with less power loss, using transformers to step voltages up and down.

facts.question: What machine was Ada Lovelace's algorithm designed for?
facts.explanation: The daughter of poet Lord Byron, Ada Lovelace saw potential in Babbage's machine beyond mere calculation. Her notes on the Analytical Engine included what is recognized as the first algorithm intended for machine processing, making her a computing pioneer.

facts.question: What English phrase comes from Alexander the Great's solution to an ancient puzzle?
distractors: Between a rock and a hard place
facts.explanation: The knot was said to be tied by King Gordius and held a yoke to a pole in the ancient city of Gordium. Various accounts differ on whether Alexander cut it with his sword or found a trick (pulling out the lynch pin). Either way, his solution gave us the phrase 'cutting the Gordian knot' — meaning solving an impossible problem through bold, unconventional action rather than patient analysis.

facts.question: Who was Alexander the Great's famous tutor?
facts.explanation: Tutored by Aristotle, Alexander never lost a battle. His conquests spread Greek culture (Hellenism) across the known world. He founded over 20 cities named Alexandria and died in Babylon in 323 BCE, aged 32.

facts.question: For how many years were the ancient Olympic Games held?
facts.explanation: Originally a religious festival honoring Zeus, the games featured events like chariot racing, wrestling, and the stadion foot race. A sacred truce halted wars during the games, and victors received olive wreaths.

facts.question: What was the Antikythera Mechanism used to predict?
facts.explanation: Recovered from a Roman-era shipwreck off the Greek island of Antikythera in 1901, this intricate bronze device contained at least 30 meshing gears and could track the movements of the Sun, Moon, and planets.

facts.question: What did the Apollo 13 crew use as a lifeboat after the explosion?
facts.explanation: Commander Jim Lovell's famous words, 'Houston, we've had a problem,' began one of NASA's finest hours. Engineers on the ground improvised a CO2 scrubber from duct tape and cardboard to keep the crew alive in the cramped module.

facts.question: How many warships did the cat Unsinkable Sam survive the sinking of during WWII?
facts.explanation: Originally a German cat aboard the Bismarck, he was found floating on a board after the ship sank in 1941. British sailors rescued him and put him on HMS Cossack, which was torpedoed five months later — Sam survived again. He was transferred to HMS Ark Royal, which was also torpedoed. After his third sinking, the Royal Navy decided Sam had earned his retirement on dry land.

facts.question: Where did the Attack on Pearl Harbor take place?
facts.explanation: In 1941, the clash at Pearl Harbor changed the course of history!

facts.question: How much does the Aztec Sun Stone weigh?
facts.explanation: The stone displays the five Aztec 'suns' (world ages), the current sun god Tonatiuh at center, and the 20 day signs of the sacred calendar. It was buried after the Spanish conquest and rediscovered in 1790 in Mexico City's main square.

facts.question: What did archaeologists discover in Mexico City in 2017 that confirmed Spanish conquistador accounts?
facts.answer: An Aztec skull tower containing over 600 human skulls
distractors: An underground temple filled with gold offerings
A mass grave of Spanish soldiers from the conquest
An ancient aqueduct system larger than Roman ones
A library of Aztec codices thought to be destroyed
facts.explanation: The tzompantli (skull rack) was a central feature of Aztec religious practice. Victims of ritual sacrifice had their skulls displayed on wooden racks and eventually cemented into towers. The 2017 discovery near the Templo Mayor in Mexico City shocked researchers because it included women and children — suggesting the practice was even more widespread than previously thought.

facts.question: In what year did the Barrios' War of Reunification happen?
facts.explanation: Barrios' War of Reunification: 1885 war in Central America.

facts.question: When did the Battle of Coronel occur?

facts.explanation: In 1866, the clash at Custoza changed the course of history!

facts.explanation: Battle of France: Nazi German invasion of France in 1940. It took place in the region of Belgium.

facts.question: What was the Battle of Gvozd Mountain?
facts.explanation: Battle of Gvozd Mountain: 1097 middle ages battle. It took place in the region of Croatia.

facts.question: When did the Battle of Hastings occur?
facts.explanation: In 1066, the clash at Battle changed the course of history!

facts.question: Who won the Battle of Hastings in 1066?
facts.explanation: William, Duke of Normandy, claimed the English throne and invaded with about 7,000 troops. Harold was killed (possibly by an arrow to the eye), and the Norman Conquest fundamentally changed English language, law, and culture.

facts.question: What was the Battle of Hattin?
facts.answer: Victory of Saladin against Crusader states, 1187
facts.explanation: Battle of Hattin: Victory of Saladin against Crusader states, 1187. It took place in the region of Horns of Hattin.

facts.question: When did the Battle of Ichikawa-Funabashi occur?
facts.explanation: In 1868, the clash at Ichikawa changed the course of history!

facts.question: In which region did the Battle of Kelja take place?

facts.question: Where did the Battle of Königsberg take place?

facts.question: When did the Battle of La Marfée occur?
facts.explanation: Battle of La Marfée: 1641 battle of the Franco-Spanish War of 1635–1659. It took place in the region of Sedan.

facts.question: In what year did the Battle of Magenta happen?
facts.explanation: Battle of Magenta: 1859 battle of the Second Italian War of Independence. It took place in the region of Magenta.

facts.question: How many Japanese aircraft carriers were sunk at the Battle of Midway?
facts.explanation: American codebreakers had decrypted Japanese plans, enabling an ambush. The loss of four fleet carriers — Akagi, Kaga, Soryu, and Hiryu — along with their experienced pilots was a blow from which Japan's navy never recovered.

facts.question: When did the Battle of Milne Bay occur?
facts.explanation: In 1942, the clash at Milne Bay changed the course of history!

facts.question: When did the Battle of Mogadishu occur?

facts.explanation: In 1422, the clash at Central Bohemian Region changed the course of history!

facts.question: Where did the Battle of New Orleans take place?
facts.explanation: In 1812, the clash at Jean Lafitte National Historical Park and Preserve changed the course of history!

facts.question: Where did the Battle of Noreia take place?
facts.explanation: Battle of Noreia: 113 BCE battle of the Cimbrian War. It took place in the region of Noreia.

facts.question: Where did the Battle of Novara take place?
facts.explanation: Battle of Novara: 1849 battle during the First Italian War of Independence. It took place in the region of Novara.

facts.question: Where did the Battle of Palmito Ranch take place?
facts.explanation: In 1865, the clash at Cameron County changed the course of history!

facts.question: When did the Battle of Pavia occur?
facts.explanation: In 1525, the clash at Pavia changed the course of history!

facts.question: In what year did the Battle of Poitiers happen?
facts.explanation: Battle of Poitiers: 1356 battle of the Hundred Years' War. It took place in the region of Nouaillé-Maupertuis.

facts.question: Where did the Battle of Rajovka take place?
facts.explanation: In 1708, the clash at Ukraine changed the course of history!

facts.question: Where did the Battle of Saint-Omer take place?
facts.explanation: In 1340, the clash at Saint-Omer changed the course of history!

facts.question: When did the Battle of Sekigahara occur?
facts.explanation: Battle of Sekigahara: 1600 battle preceding Tokugawa shogunate in Japan. It took place in the region of Gifu.

facts.question: Where did the Battle of Soissons take place?
facts.explanation: In 486, the clash at Soissons changed the course of history!

facts.question: Approximately how many total casualties occurred at the Battle of Stalingrad?
facts.explanation: The Soviet defense and counterattack at Stalingrad destroyed the German 6th Army and marked the turning point of WWII on the Eastern Front. Street-by-street urban combat in freezing conditions made it devastatingly brutal.

facts.question: Where did the Battle of Stony Point take place?
facts.explanation: In 1779, the clash at Stony Point changed the course of history!

facts.question: When did the Battle of Struth occur?
facts.explanation: This 1945 event left a lasting mark on world history!

facts.question: Where did the Battle of the Dukla Pass take place?
facts.explanation: In 1944, the clash at Dukla Pass changed the course of history!

facts.question: Where did the Battle of the Golden Spurs take place?
facts.explanation: In 1302, the clash at Kortrijk changed the course of history!

facts.question: In what year did the Battle of the Hydaspes happen?
facts.explanation: Battle of the Hydaspes: battle fought by Alexander the Great in 326 BCE against King Porus of the Paurava kingdom on the banks of the Hydaspes River (Jhelum River) in the Punjab near Bhera.

facts.question: Where did the Battle of the Persian Gate on the Mountain?
facts.explanation: The dramatic events near Persian Gates reshaped the region forever!

facts.question: Where did the Battle of the Silarius River take place?
facts.explanation: Battle of the Silarius River: last battle of the Roman Servile War, 71 BC. It took place in the region of Sele.

facts.question: Where did the Battle of the Teutoburg Forest take place?
facts.explanation: The dramatic events near Osnabrück reshaped the region forever!

facts.question: When did the Battle of Tinian occur?
facts.explanation: Battle of Tinian: 1944 battle of the Pacific campaign of World War II on the island of Tinian in the Mariana Islands.

facts.question: Where did the Battle of Turaida take place?
facts.explanation: In 1298, the clash at Gauja changed the course of history!

facts.question: In what year did the Battle of Wanjialing happen?
facts.explanation: Battle of Wanjialing: 1938 battle in China.

facts.question: When did the Battle of Waterloo occur?
facts.explanation: In 1815, the clash at Waterloo Battlefield changed the course of history!

facts.question: When did the Battle of Westkapelle occur?
facts.explanation: This 1253 event left a lasting mark on world history!

facts.question: In what year did the Battle of Yan Province happen?
facts.explanation: Battle of Yan Province: battles fought between warlords Cao Cao and Lü Bu (194-195). It took place in the region of Yan Province. This occurred in 0194.

facts.question: In what year did the Battle of Ye happen?
facts.explanation: Battle of Ye: battle between warlords Cao Cao and Yuan Shang (204).

facts.question: In which region did the Battle of Zelva take place?
facts.explanation: Battle of Zelva: 1792 battle. It took place in the region of Zeĺva.

facts.question: Which Spartan king led the defense at Thermopylae?
facts.explanation: The Greeks held the narrow coastal pass for three days before being outflanked via a mountain path. Though a military defeat, the stand inspired Greek unity and contributed to eventual Persian defeat at Salamis and Plataea.

facts.question: In which region did the Battles of Saratoga take place?
facts.explanation: Battles of Saratoga: 1777 major turning point of the American Revolutionary War. It took place in the region of Stillwater.

facts.question: How long did the Bay of Pigs invasion last before the exiles were defeated?
facts.explanation: About 1,400 exiles landed at the Bay of Pigs but were quickly defeated by Castro's forces within three days. The fiasco embarrassed the Kennedy administration and pushed Cuba closer to the Soviet Union, contributing to the Cuban Missile Crisis.

facts.question: Did Benjamin Franklin actually propose the turkey as America's national bird?
facts.answer: No — he only made a joking comparison in a private letter to his daughter
distractors: Yes — he formally proposed it at the Constitutional Convention
Yes — he submitted a written petition to Congress
No — he wanted the American bison instead
Yes — but the proposal was voted down unanimously
facts.explanation: The myth that Franklin wanted the turkey as the national symbol is one of America's most persistent misquotes. In his 1784 letter to Sarah Bache, he criticized the Society of the Cincinnati's eagle insignia as looking like a turkey, then went on a tangent about how turkeys were brave birds (they'd attack a British soldier) while eagles were lazy thieves. He never made any formal proposal.

facts.question: What bacterium caused the Black Death?
facts.explanation: Caused by the bacterium Yersinia pestis, the plague arrived in Europe via merchant ships from the Black Sea. It wiped out roughly one-third to one-half of Europe's population, reshaping economies, labor markets, and social structures.

facts.question: In what year did the Byzantine–Bulgarian War of 913–927 happen?
facts.explanation: Byzantine–Bulgarian War of 913–927: war between the Byzantine Empire and the First Bulgarian Empire. It took place in the region of Balkans. This occurred in 0913.

facts.question: What bizarre event happened at the 'Cadaver Synod' of 897 CE?
facts.answer: A dead pope was exhumed, dressed in robes, and put on trial
distractors: A live pope was mistakenly declared dead and buried
Two popes fought a duel in the Vatican
A pope's pet bear was tried for attacking a cardinal
A pope excommunicated an entire country's dead population
facts.explanation: The Cadaver Synod (Synodus Horrenda) was driven by political revenge. Stephen VI blamed Formosus for political failures. The rotting corpse was dressed, placed on a throne, and 'answered' charges through a teenage deacon standing behind the throne. After conviction, the body was stripped, mutilated, and thrown into the Tiber River. Public outrage was so great that Stephen VI was later deposed, imprisoned, and strangled.

facts.question: How many members made up the Cambridge spy ring?
facts.explanation: The group — Kim Philby, Donald Maclean, Guy Burgess, Anthony Blunt, and John Cairncross — infiltrated MI5, MI6, and the Foreign Office. Their betrayal compromised Western intelligence operations throughout the Cold War.

facts.question: How did Captain Cook trick his sailors into eating sauerkraut to prevent scurvy?
facts.answer: He served it only to officers and told the crew it was too good for them
distractors: He mixed it into their beer so they couldn't taste it
He told them it was a secret German strength potion
He offered gold coins to anyone who ate a full portion
He disguised it as a new type of tropical fruit
facts.explanation: Scurvy had killed more sailors than warfare, storms, and shipwrecks combined. Cook experimented with many anti-scorbutic foods, but sauerkraut (rich in vitamin C) was his standby. When sailors refused to eat it, Cook had it served exclusively at the officers' table. Within a week, the crew petitioned for their 'fair share.' Cook's sauerkraut trick helped him complete three Pacific voyages with remarkably low mortality.

facts.question: In what year did the Castille War happen?
facts.explanation: Castille War: 1578 war between Brunei and Spain.

facts.question: Where did the Catalan–Genoese War take place?
facts.explanation: Catalan–Genoese War: 1330–1336 war. It took place in the region of Western Mediterranean.

facts.question: What caused the disastrous Charge of the Light Brigade?
facts.explanation: During the Crimean War, a vague order led Lord Cardigan to lead his light cavalry against a heavily fortified Russian gun position. Nearly 300 men were killed or wounded in the disastrous charge, immortalized in Tennyson's poem.

facts.question: What spontaneously happened between German and British soldiers on Christmas Day 1914?
facts.answer: They stopped fighting, exchanged gifts, and played football in no man's land
distractors: Both sides launched surprise attacks expecting the other to be celebrating
German soldiers surrendered en masse as a Christmas gesture
British commanders invited German officers to a formal dinner
Both sides agreed to a permanent ceasefire that lasted until spring
facts.explanation: The Christmas Truce was not organized by any command — it bubbled up from soldiers on both sides. In some sectors, men exchanged cigarettes, buttons, and food. Joint burial of the dead occurred. At least one football match was documented. Commanders on both sides were furious and issued strict orders against any future fraternization. Nothing like it ever happened again in the war.

facts.question: How many languages did Cleopatra reportedly speak?
facts.explanation: Cleopatra was a polyglot who spoke nine languages. She formed political and romantic alliances with Julius Caesar and Mark Antony, making Egypt a powerful player in Roman politics before Octavian's conquest.

facts.question: Who wrote that 'if Cleopatra's nose had been shorter, the whole face of the world would have been changed'?
facts.explanation: Pascal's point was that if Cleopatra had been less attractive, she might not have seduced Julius Caesar and Mark Antony, Rome might not have had its civil wars, and Western civilization could have developed entirely differently. This became one of the most famous arguments about whether history is shaped by great forces or by random personal quirks.

facts.question: How many laws does the Code of Hammurabi contain?
facts.explanation: Inscribed on a 2.25-meter basalt stele, the code contains 282 laws covering commerce, property, family, and criminal justice. It established the principle of 'an eye for an eye' and presumption of innocence.

facts.question: In what year did the Berlin Wall fall?
facts.explanation: Built by East Germany to prevent citizens from fleeing to the West, the Wall became the most potent symbol of the Iron Curtain. Its fall on November 9, 1989, marked the beginning of German reunification.

facts.question: In what year did the Colombian-Peruvian War happen?
facts.explanation: Colombian-Peruvian War: armed conflict. It took place in the region of Leticia. This occurred in 1932.

facts.question: What was the magnetic compass originally used for in China?
facts.explanation: Early compasses used lodestone (magnetite) shaped into a spoon on a bronze plate. By the Song Dynasty (11th century), compass navigation enabled Chinese maritime exploration. The technology later spread to Europe via Arab traders.

facts.question: When did the Umayyad Arabs besiege Constantinople?
facts.explanation: A year-long siege that saved Byzantine Christianity and shaped medieval Europe's future.

facts.question: How many days did the Cuban Missile Crisis last?
facts.explanation: The crisis began when US reconnaissance discovered Soviet nuclear missiles being installed in Cuba. President Kennedy imposed a naval blockade. Soviet Premier Khrushchev ultimately agreed to remove the missiles in exchange for a US pledge not to invade Cuba.

facts.question: How many Allied troops landed on D-Day?
facts.explanation: Codenamed Operation Overlord, the invasion opened a second front in Western Europe against Nazi Germany. Five beaches — Utah, Omaha, Gold, Juno, and Sword — were assaulted simultaneously.

facts.question: What did the bear Wojtek do at the Battle of Monte Cassino?
facts.answer: Carried ammunition boxes for the Polish Army
distractors: Charged at German soldiers to frighten them
Pulled supply wagons through mountain passes
Detected landmines with his sense of smell
Stood guard at the command post entrance
facts.explanation: Polish soldiers adopted the bear cub in Iran in 1942. To get him on a British transport ship, they officially enlisted him with a name, rank, and serial number. At Monte Cassino, he genuinely carried 100-pound ammunition crates. He enjoyed wrestling soldiers, taking showers, and drinking beer. The Polish 22nd Transport Company adopted his image as their unit emblem — a bear carrying an artillery shell.

facts.question: How did Louis XIV use ballet as a tool of political control?
facts.answer: Dance ability at court determined political power, keeping nobles busy and competing
distractors: He banned anyone who couldn't dance from entering Paris
He taxed those who refused to attend ballet performances
He used ballet codes to send secret military orders
He required all laws to be announced through dance performances
facts.explanation: Louis established the first professional ballet academy in 1661. At Versailles, courtiers were judged by their dancing ability — a poor performance could end a political career. The elaborate dance steps were so complex that nobles spent hours daily practicing. This kept the aristocracy too busy and too focused on court performance to plot against the king. It was social control through choreography.

facts.question: What happened during the Dancing Plague of Strasbourg in 1518?
facts.answer: About 400 people danced uncontrollably, some dying from exhaustion
distractors: A festival celebration got out of control for three days
A new dance style became so popular that people skipped work
A religious sect performed ritual dances for a week
A musician played so well that crowds danced for hours
facts.explanation: The authorities initially encouraged the dancing by hiring musicians and building a stage, thinking it would help them get it out of their systems. It didn't. Modern theories range from mass psychogenic illness (stress-induced hysteria) to ergot poisoning from contaminated grain, which can cause convulsions and hallucinations. The event is well-documented in city records and physician accounts.

facts.question: Where were the Dead Sea Scrolls discovered?
facts.explanation: A Bedouin shepherd stumbled upon the first scrolls in a cave. The collection includes over 900 texts dating from the 3rd century BCE to the 1st century CE, revolutionizing understanding of ancient Judaism and early Christianity.

facts.question: How many major 'Defenestrations of Prague' sparked major wars?
facts.answer: Two — in 1419 (Hussite Wars) and 1618 (Thirty Years' War)
distractors: One — in 1618 triggering the Thirty Years' War
Four — one per century from 1419 to 1719
Five — starting in 1348 and ending in 1848
facts.explanation: In 1419, Hussite protestors threw Catholic city councilors out of the New Town Hall windows to their deaths, sparking the Hussite Wars. In 1618, Protestant nobles threw two Catholic governors and their secretary out of Prague Castle's 70-foot windows — remarkably, all three survived by landing in a dung heap. Catholics called it a miracle; Protestants said it was just a big pile of manure. The incident triggered the devastating Thirty Years' War.

facts.question: In what year did the Battle of Deutschbrod take place?
facts.explanation: Medieval Bohemia clash during the fiery Hussite Wars revolution!

facts.question: How were the Moai statues most likely transported to their platforms?
facts.explanation: The Rapa Nui people carved these monolithic statues, averaging 4 meters tall and 12 tonnes, to honor ancestral chiefs. Researchers discovered they were 'walked' to their platforms using rocking motions and ropes — not rolled on logs as once believed.

facts.question: What was the Edigu's campaign against Moscow?
facts.answer: 1408 siege of Moscow by Golden Horde
facts.explanation: Edigu's campaign against Moscow: 1408 siege of Moscow by Golden Horde.

facts.question: In what year was the Emancipation Proclamation issued?
facts.explanation: While it did not immediately free anyone (it applied only to rebelling states), it transformed the Civil War into a fight against slavery and allowed African Americans to serve in the Union Army. About 180,000 Black soldiers eventually served.

facts.question: What happened when the Australian military used machine guns against emus in 1932?
facts.answer: The emus proved too elusive and the military withdrew in defeat
distractors: The emus were completely wiped out within a week
The emus attacked the soldiers and destroyed their equipment
The campaign was canceled when emus were reclassified as protected
The soldiers refused orders because they liked the emus
facts.explanation: Farmers had complained about 20,000 emus trampling wheat fields after WWI soldier-settlers established farms in emu territory. The Royal Australian Artillery was sent with machine guns, but emus scattered in small groups, ran at 30 mph, and shrugged off multiple bullets. The commander compared them to Zulu warriors for their evasive tactics. The 'Great Emu War' remains one of history's most absurd military campaigns.

facts.question: What was the name of the electro-mechanical device Turing built to crack Enigma?
facts.explanation: The Enigma machine was considered unbreakable by Germany. Turing built the Bombe, an electro-mechanical device that rapidly tested Enigma settings, giving the Allies vital intelligence known as Ultra.

facts.question: What components of the Enigma machine created its encryption?
facts.answer: Rotating cipher discs (rotors) and a plugboard
facts.explanation: The machine used rotating cipher discs (rotors) and a plugboard to encrypt messages. Its apparent security lay in the astronomically large number of configurations, but repeated patterns and operator errors gave codebreakers crucial footholds.

facts.question: When did the Falklands War occur?
facts.explanation: Falklands War: undeclared war between Argentina and the United Kingdom in 1982. It took place in the region of Falkland Islands.

facts.question: What was the name of the massive cannon used to breach Constantinople's walls?
facts.explanation: Constantinople had withstood sieges for over 1,000 years. Mehmed used massive cannons — including the 8-meter 'Basilica' — to break the Theodosian Walls. The city became Istanbul, capital of the Ottoman Empire.

facts.question: Where did the First Battle of al-Faw take place?
facts.explanation: In 1986, the clash at Al-Faw Peninsula changed the course of history!

facts.question: In what year did the First Battle of St Albans happen?
facts.explanation: First Battle of St Albans: 1455 conflict. It took place in the region of St Albans.

facts.question: When did the First conflict in the Goryeo–Khitan Wars occur?
facts.explanation: The First conflict in the Goryeo–Khitan Wars was a notable historical conflict. It occurred in Korean Peninsula. It took place in 0993.

facts.question: When did the First Macedonian War occur?
facts.explanation: First Macedonian War: war between Rome and Macedonia. This occurred in 213 BCE.

facts.question: What was the First Punic War?
facts.answer: First War between Rome and Carthage
facts.explanation: First Punic War: First War between Rome and Carthage. It took place in the region of Mediterranean Sea. This occurred in 263 BCE.

facts.question: In what year did the First Rohilla War happen?
facts.explanation: First Rohilla War: Military campaign in 1773–1774.

facts.question: What type of innovative diagram did Florence Nightingale create?
facts.explanation: Her innovative 'coxcomb' polar area diagrams showed that preventable diseases caused far more deaths than combat. Her reforms reduced the death rate at Scutari Hospital from 42% to 2%, founding modern nursing and medical statistics.

facts.question: What event on July 14, 1789, is considered the start of the French Revolution?
facts.explanation: Driven by economic hardship, inequality, and Enlightenment ideas, the revolution overthrew the monarchy, established a republic, and introduced the Declaration of the Rights of Man. It profoundly influenced modern democracy worldwide.

facts.question: Why was Galileo tried by the Roman Inquisition?
facts.explanation: Galileo's telescopic observations of Jupiter's moons and Venus's phases supported Copernicus's theory that the Earth orbited the Sun. The Church found him 'vehemently suspect of heresy' and placed him under house arrest for life.

facts.question: How fast could a message travel through Genghis Khan's Yam postal system?
facts.explanation: The Yam system had over 1,400 relay stations across Asia and Europe, each stocked with fresh horses, food, and shelter. Riders carried a gerege (passport tablet) and had absolute right-of-way. The system was so effective that Marco Polo called it the best postal service in the world. It functioned for over 150 years and was faster than anything Europe had until the 19th century.

facts.question: What was the Ghost Dance War?
facts.answer: Armed conflict between a group of the Sioux people and the US from 1890 until 1891
facts.explanation: Ghost Dance War: armed conflict between a group of the Sioux people and the US from 1890 until 1891. It took place in the region of South Dakota.

facts.question: How fast did the wave of molasses travel during Boston's Great Molasses Flood of 1919?
facts.explanation: The Purity Distilling Company had built a shoddily constructed 50-foot-tall tank holding 2.3 million gallons of molasses. When it burst, the sticky wave demolished buildings, tore a fire station from its foundation, and even knocked a train off its tracks. Cleanup took weeks, and the tragedy led to stricter building codes and engineering oversight in Massachusetts.

facts.question: Which dynasty built most of the existing Great Wall?
facts.explanation: Contrary to popular belief, the Wall is not visible from space with the naked eye. Most of the existing wall was built during the Ming Dynasty (1368–1644). It served as defense, border control, and a trade regulation tool.

facts.question: Why did Erik the Red name an ice-covered island 'Greenland'?
facts.answer: As a marketing trick to attract settlers
distractors: Because it was actually green when he arrived during a warm period
The original Norse word meant 'grey land' not 'green land'
He was color-blind and confused the ice with grass
It was named after his ship, the Green Dragon
facts.explanation: Erik the Red was banished from Iceland for murder and discovered Greenland around 985 CE. He chose the name 'Greenland' because, as the sagas record, 'he said that people would be more eager to go there if the land had an attractive name.'
